Most Creative
Session Awards

Session Awards

10 Awards
🏆
Most Valuable Player (MVP)
Bob
For displaying the guile of a master thief and the aerial grace of a very small, very hairy bat. The party's pockets are heavy with gold and the mission is a success entirely due to Bob’s nimble fingers.
Runner-Up: Moor
🏆
Best One-Liner
Moor
For a meta-commentary so sharp it cut through the table talk and established a legendary character trait in ten words or less.
Runner-Up: Opal
🏆
Best Tactical Call
Lavender
For keeping a cool head while her lungs were filled with poison, proving that the best solution to a lethal problem is often just a very strong breeze.
Runner-Up: Aramil
🏆
Most Dramatic Moment
Aramil
For a brush with death so intimate it required a literal miracle from the Cleric to bring him back from the brink of the afterlife.
Runner-Up: Bob
🏆
Most Damage in a Single Turn
Opal
For proving that a Goliath’s fury is the most efficient way to turn a 40-foot apex predator into several 5-foot appetizers.
Runner-Up: Lavender
🏆
Most In-Character Moment
Aramil
For maintaining the bitter, academic resentment of an underpaid adjunct even while being pursued by prehistoric monsters.
Runner-Up: Opal
🏆
Heart of the Party
Moor
For being the quiet, scaled anchor of the group. Without Moor, this adventure would have ended at the first pressure plate.
Runner-Up: Lavender
🏆
Most Chaotic Action
Opal
For refusing to let a minor inconvenience like 'total blindness' stop her from wrestling with furniture in the middle of a dungeon.
Runner-Up: Bob
🏆
Best Use of a Spell or Ability
Bob
For identifying the exact loophole in the lighting conditions to transform a Halfling into a stealth-bomber.
Runner-Up: Aramil
🏆
Most Creative Problem Solving
Lavender
For realizing that the treasure wasn't just loot—it was a highly effective and very expensive paperweight for murder traps.
Runner-Up: Opal
Biggest Moment

The Biggest Moment

45/50 Impact Score
The Biggest Moment
Aramil secretly pried the golden eye from the Snake
Aramil secretly pried the golden eye from the Snake Idol as the party boarded the canoes to leave, plunging the entire continent of Faerun into eternal darkness.
ARAMIL
45
OUT OF 50
They had done it. Against odds that the DM himself admitted he thought were insurmountable, Charisma Company had cleared the Temple of the Man and Crocodile, survived a T-Rex stirring awake beneath them, weathered a fireball from a rival party, and sprinted through a real-time skill challenge with four minutes and twenty seconds on the clock. They had collapsed into Fizzleblast's safety bubble as the undead clawed out of the Chultan soil behind them, and the gnome wizard snored through the entire apocalypse outside his force field. The tension was gone. The dice were down. The session was in its epilogue -- players comparing loot, laughing about close calls, marveling that they were the first party in the DM's run of this one-shot to get everyone out alive. It was, in every way, the exhale after the held breath.
Aramil's player asked about the Eye of the Serpent -- a golden gem set into the Snake Idol that Bob had already stashed in his pack. The DM confirmed the gem was accessible. "I rolled an 18 on the sleight of hand to take it," Aramil's player said, almost casually. "As we're getting in the boat, I'm going to be like..." The DM processed it for a beat. "As you pull it out, nobody sees you taking it. The other eye is still intact. Nobody sees it because Blast is asleep. He doesn't know that you took it." A pause. Then: "And you have plunged the entire continent into eternal darkness." The table went silent for exactly one second before the reactions hit. The DM continued, his voice carrying the weight of someone who had been waiting for this moment the entire session: "The sun will not rise again in Faerun."
Impact Scores
Narrative10
Irrevrsbl9
Cascade10
Emotional9
Improbblty7
Trigger
A deliberate, secret player decision. No dice forced this. No DM reveal prompted it. Aramil saw the idol, saw the gem, and chose to take it -- alone, unobserved, without consulting the party.
When
The very end of the session -- after the party had survived the temple, the T-Rex, the rival adventurers, and the skill challenge escape. The mission was over. They were safe.
Who
Aramil (Half-Elf Warlock), played by Kyle 2
Honorable Mentions
35
/ 50
Opal Kills the Giant Constrictor Snake While Blind
After rolling a Natural 1 on a mirror puzzle and taking 14 radiant damage directly to her eyes, Opal spent most of the mid-session completely blind. When the Giant Constrictor Snake emerged and began squeezing Aramil to -10 HP, Opal -- still unable to see -- rolled a critical hit with her Great Axe for 30 damage, chopping through the coiled layers of the snake like a cake and killing it outright. She freed the unconscious Aramil and ended the fight in a single swing she could not see.
31
/ 50
Moor Melts the Gorallon with the Thermal Mirror
When two Gorallons (four-armed jungle apes) charged the party at the temple entrance, Moor had a flash of tactical brilliance: instead of fighting them conventionally, he asked to turn one of the heliographic thermal mirrors and aim it directly at the creature. He rolled an 18 on the Strength check to rotate the massive mirror, and the focused beam of concentrated sunlight burned a hole clean through the Gorallon's torso, melting it from the inside out. The DM described the smell as "cooked steak plus burnt hair." This was the first creative use of the temple's environment, and it set the template the party would use for the rest of the session.
36
/ 50
The Sun Goes Down and the Rival Party Launches a Fireball
Just as Bob successfully snatched the Golden Snake Idol from the archway above the sleeping T-Rex -- nailing stealth checks with a Dirty 20 and a 21 -- the rival adventuring party launched a Fireball from above. Bob took 9 damage (halved by his Dex save of 24), but the real consequence was that the Fireball hit the T-Rex at full force, waking it in a rage while simultaneously destroying its nest and the remaining mirror infrastructure. This triggered the real-time skill challenge: four minutes and twenty seconds to reach Fizzleblast's bubble, with a death on the line at three failures. The session's entire climax -- the frantic escape, the pressure plate blades, the rope-pulling, the dimension-dooring mages -- all cascaded from this single ambush.
Post-Session Interviews

Post-Session Interviews

Here are the post-session character interviews for Charisma Company, conducted by Elswyth Pattle, the field chronicler for Mercenaries, Murders, and Mothers-in-Law Monthly.
✎ These interviews are AI-generated fiction based on each character's actions, personality, and dialogue during the session. The characters didn't actually say these words — but they definitely would have.
Moor7 questions
Lavender6 questions
Aramil6 questions
Opal6 questions
Bob6 questions
M
Moor
Dragonborn Cleric
7 Q&A
▼
Setting
Moor is found sitting heavily on a log near the beached canoes. He has removed his helmet and is methodically cleaning a mixture of gorilla gore and snake slime off his mace with a rag. He looks like a parent who has just survived a toddlers' birthday party at a trampoline park. He does not look up as Elswyth approaches.
Q
Elswyth Pattle
Master Moor? Thank you for sitting with me. That was... quite the extraction. How are you feeling?
Moor
“Tired. Sticky.”
Q
Elswyth Pattle
You seemed to take on the role of protector for the group. Is that a calling of Tyr, or just your nature?
Moor
“Necessity. Someone has to stand in front. Someone has to carry the shield. If I don't, they die. They are squishy. I am scales. It is simple math.”
Q
Elswyth Pattle
Let’s talk about the Temple. The "Man and Crocodile" puzzle. You and Lavender had to coordinate perfectly to cross those pressure plates. What was going through your mind during that?
Moor
“She is... light. Very light. I was worried the plate would not click. Then I was worried she would step too fast. It was a trust exercise. I do not like trust exercises. I prefer hammers. Hammers are predictable. Tieflings with genies are not.”
Q
Elswyth Pattle
You had a significant moment with the undead—the skeletons in the gas room. You turned almost all of them to dust instantly.
Moor
“Tyr was watching. The scales must be balanced. Undead are heavy on the wrong side. I simply... removed the weight. It was efficient. I like efficiency.”
Q
Elswyth Pattle
Speaking of efficiency, you healed your companions repeatedly. Aramil went down, Opal was blind... is it frustrating?
Moor
“It is the job. Aramil talks. I heal. Opal smashes. I heal. Bob... Bob is Bob. I heal. If I stop, the talking and smashing stops. Then it is just quiet. I like quiet. But I do not like dead friends. So, I heal.”
Q
Elswyth Pattle
Do you have any regrets about how the expedition went?
Moor
“We ran too much. We left loot. But... we are breathing. Regret is for the dead.”
Q
Elswyth Pattle
And what about the artifact? Aramil seems to have secured... something.
Moor
“Aramil found a shiny thing. He is happy. I hope the shiny thing does not bite.”
Closing
Moor stands up, his joints popping audibly. He places the clean mace back in its loop. He pats Elswyth gently on the head with a massive hand, almost knocking her over, and walks away toward the fire without another word.
L
Lavender
Tiefling Warlock
6 Q&A
▼
Setting
The interior of the ring is opulent—pillows, silks, and the smell of expensive incense. Lavender is lounging on a velvet chaise, sipping a glass of wine that definitely shouldn't exist in a jungle. Her imp, Tika, is currently a raven, preening on her shoulder. She looks pristine, despite the humidity.
Q
Elswyth Pattle
Miss Lavender, thank you for inviting me in. It’s... much nicer in here than out there.
Lavender
“Darling, everywhere is nicer than out there. Did you see the humidity? But yes, welcome to the VIP lounge.”
Q
Elswyth Pattle
You seemed to handle the magical traps with a certain... flair. Specifically, using the Gust of Wind to clear the gas. That was quick thinking.
Lavender
“Well, obviously. I mean, the boys were all hacking and coughing and looking dreadful. Someone had to be the thermostat. Besides, Tika told me the air was wretched. I trust Tika more than I trust anyone with a beard in this party.”
Q
Elswyth Pattle
Speaking of Tika, your familiar took quite a hit from that giant snake. He... died, didn't he?
Lavender
“He did. It was rude. Extremely rude of the snake. Tika is family. You don't eat family. But... we have an arrangement, Jezebel and I. He comes back. He always comes back. But I made sure that snake burned for it. Did you see the Hellish Rebuke? It matched my outfit perfectly.”
Q
Elswyth Pattle
You and Opal seem close. You were guiding her when she was blind.
Lavender
“Oh, Opal. She’s a sweetie, isn’t she? Big as a house, heart of a... bigger house. When she couldn’t see, she looked so small. I couldn’t have that. A blind Goliath is just a tragedy waiting to happen. Plus, holding hands is nice. Keeps her from walking into laser beams.”
Q
Elswyth Pattle
You ended up immune to fire thanks to that Zephyr Reliquary you found. That required removing the room's cooling system, which woke the snake. Any regrets?
Lavender
“Regrets? Darling, look at me. I walked through fire. I looked like a goddess. The snake was going to wake up anyway—it’s a dungeon. Everything wakes up and tries to kill you. I just made sure I looked hot—but not too hot—while doing it.”
Q
Elswyth Pattle
The sun hasn't risen. It's pitch black outside. Does that worry you?
Lavender
“It’s a mood. Very gothic chic. I’m sure it’s fine. Aramil did something nerdy, Bob did something sneaky, and now it’s dark. We’ll fix it after a nap. Or we won’t. I have darkvision.”
Closing
Lavender drains her glass and snaps her fingers. The raven caws. She offers Elswyth a chocolate from a silver bowl, winks, and then pointedly picks up a sleeping mask, signaling the interview is over.
A
Aramil
Half-Elf Warlock
6 Q&A
▼
Setting
Aramil is crouched in the shadows behind the supply crates, frantically scribbling in a leather-bound journal. He looks gaunt, his fedora is stained with sweat and soot, and he keeps checking over his shoulder. He clutches a heavy satchel to his chest. He looks like a man vibrating with caffeine and paranoia.
Q
Elswyth Pattle
Professor? Or is it Mr. Aramil?
Aramil
“Adjunct Professor Emeritus! Or... just Aramil is fine for the paperwork. Just don't put "Assistant." My father reads these, you know.”
Q
Elswyth Pattle
Right. You seemed very focused on the murals and the history of the temple. Did you find what you were looking for?
Aramil
“[He lowers his voice, leaning in.] I found vindication, Elswyth. This isn't just a "temple." It's a complex solar-locking mechanism! The Man and the Crocodile? It's metaphor! Symbology! The others, they just see loot. I see history. I see tenure.”
Q
Elswyth Pattle
You tried to speak to the giant constrictor snake. You called it "Brother." That was... a bold strategy.
Aramil
“It was a diplomatic attempt! I speak with animals! Usually they are more conversational. "Where is the nut?" "I saw a hawk." This one was... rude. Philistine. It didn't appreciate the cultural significance of not eating me. But I survived! I survived to document it.”
Q
Elswyth Pattle
Let's talk about the T-Rex. You went for the idol.
Aramil
“The idol belongs in a museum! Or... at the very least, in my study at the Royal Community College. It was hanging there. The T-Rex was a variable I calculated. I used Phantasmal Force. I distracted the rival party! It was brilliant! Did you see the raptors I conjured in their minds? Pure psychological warfare.”
Q
Elswyth Pattle
Aramil... the sun is gone. The entire jungle is in darkness. Do you think that has anything to do with the artifact you took?
Aramil
“Correlation does not imply causation! That is basic science! Perhaps it is... an eclipse? A very long, sudden, magical eclipse? Look, I secured the asset. The asset is safe. If the sun has decided to take a sabbatical, that is hardly my fault. Dammit Elswyth, I'm an archeologist, not a meteorologist!”
Q
Elswyth Pattle
Are you going to show Fizzleblast what you found?
Aramil
“Ha! Show him? And let him take the credit? No, no, no. This is my discovery. Aramil's Discovery. Has a ring to it, doesn't it?”
Closing
Aramil stands up abruptly, shoving the journal into his pocket. He looks wildly around the camp, pulls his fedora down low over his eyes, and scurries away toward the latrines, clutching his satchel like it contains his own heart.
O
Opal
Goliath Barbarian
6 Q&A
▼
Setting
Opal is sitting on the ground, but she is still taller than Elswyth standing up. She is currently eating a roasted lizard on a stick—the whole lizard. She looks battered, slightly singed, but beaming. She is wearing a belt that looks far too expensive for the jungle.
Q
Elswyth Pattle
Opal! You look like you've been through a war.
Opal
“[Mouth full] It was great! Did you see me? I was hewed! And jacked! I got this belt. It makes me feel like I could throw a house. Do you have a house? I could throw it.”
Q
Elswyth Pattle
Please don't. You spent a large portion of the expedition blind due to the light beam. That must have been terrifying.
Opal
“It was bad. I don't like the dark. I like to see things so I can hit them. When I couldn't see, I felt... small. Not Goliath small, but inside small. But Lavender held my hand! She has nice hands. Soft. Not like mine.”
Q
Elswyth Pattle
You did manage to kill the giant snake while blind, though. With a greataxe.
Opal
“Yeah! Chopped it like cake! A snake-cake! I just listened for the screaming. Aramil screams a lot. So I swung at the screaming and—squish! No more snake.”
Q
Elswyth Pattle
You and Bob seemed to work well together. He was... climbing on you at one point?
Opal
“Bob is like a backpack that talks! He is very small. I put him on my shoulders and we are like one big person. He finds the shiny things, I carry the heavy things. It is a good system. Though he is slippery. Very oily.”
Q
Elswyth Pattle
The run at the end—the T-Rex, the fireballs, the falling rocks. You held the gate open for everyone.
Opal
“[Nods solemnly] Everyone leaves. No one stays behind. That is the rule. Moor says we go, we go. I am strong, so I hold the door. If I don't hold the door, who holds the door? Aramil? [She laughs loudly] No. He has noodle arms. I have the quads.”
Q
Elswyth Pattle
It's dark out now, Opal. The sun didn't come back.
Opal
“Yeah. It’s weird. Maybe the sun is sleeping? Like the T-Rex? We should be quiet so we don't wake it up. But I have my belt now. If the dark comes to fight us, I will punch the dark. With reach! My tattoo lets me punch far away!”
Closing
Opal finishes the lizard in one bite, stick and all. She flexes her arm to admire her new tattoo, causing her bicep to bulge to the size of a watermelon. She offers Elswyth a charred piece of tail with a hopeful smile.
B
Bob
Halfling Rogue
6 Q&A
▼
Setting
Bob is difficult to find. Elswyth eventually locates him some fifteen feet up a tree on the perimeter of the camp. He is hanging by his knees, wearing a cloak that seems to ripple like a bat's wing. He is counting a stack of gold coins by touch in the dark.
Q
Elswyth Pattle
Bob? Can you... come down?
Bob
“No. View is better up here. Also, people can't reach my pockets. What do you want?”
Q
Elswyth Pattle
I wanted to ask about the heist. You essentially looted the entire temple while everyone else was arguing.
Bob
“Arguing takes time. Looting takes finesse. They talk about "history" and "friendship." I talk about "weight ratios" and "market value." I got the boots, I got the cloak, I got the gold. Scoreboard says Bob wins.”
Q
Elswyth Pattle
You pulled off a swap with the Scales of Justice using flasks of oil. That was incredibly risky.
Bob
“[He chuckles, a dry, rasping sound] Risk is just opportunity with bad PR. I weighed the oil. I weighed the scales. Physics doesn't lie. Magic lies, people lie, physics? Physics is my buddy. Plus, now I can... twist fate a little. Feel lucky.”
Q
Elswyth Pattle
You acted as a distraction for the pack of baboons and gorillas. You ran down the hall screaming to save the others. That... didn't seem like something you'd do for money.
Bob
“Strategic retreat. I was... leading them into a trap. Yeah. A trap. I wasn't saving anyone. Dead party members can't carry loot for me. Opal is my pack mule. If the mule gets eaten, I have to carry my own stuff. Can't have that.”
Q
Elswyth Pattle
You flew. Actual flight.
Bob
“[Voice drops an octave, very serious] I am the night. I am the wind. I am... Bob. It was cool, okay? Don't tell them I said it was cool. But jumping off that ledge? With the T-Rex screaming? Best moment of my life. Better than sex. Better than gold. Well... maybe not gold.”
Q
Elswyth Pattle
Aramil took the eye. You took the idol. Do you think you got away with it?
Bob
“Fizzleblast is an idiot. He's wearing cashmere in a swamp. He didn't see anything. But... the lights went out. Like, out out. That's usually bad. But hey, I work better in the dark. Let the tall ones worry about the sun. I got mine.”
Closing
Bob tucks the coins into a pouch that vanishes into his clothing. He releases his knees and drops fifteen feet, his cloak snapping open to slow his fall silently. He lands without a sound, winks one gleaming eye at Elswyth, and melts into the shadows of the camp.

UNIVERSAL RULES
SIP
Someone says "damage" or damage is dealt/taken
~82x
DRINK
A natural 20 is rolled or explicitly celebrated as a crit
4x
DRINK
A natural 1 is rolled or mourned as a crit fail
5x
SIP
The DM says "you see" to describe something
~32x
DRINK
The DM says "Are you sure?" or "You can certainly try"
4x
SIP
Someone mentions inventory slots or carrying capacity
~23x
SIP
The word "mirror" is spoken by anyone at the table
~41x
SIP
Someone heals or gets healed
~14x
SIP
Someone tries to loot a body or search for treasure
~22x
DRINK
An NPC or PC name is mispronounced (Aramel, Emeril, Akinquil, Allendill, Tourmaline, Advil, etc.)
14x
SIP
Someone says "10 minutes" (Brandon's favorite time increment)
~37x
DRINK
The party debates what to do instead of just doing it
~12x
SIP
A dinosaur or prehistoric creature is mentioned (T-Rex, pterodactyl, etc.)
~47x
SIP
Someone swears (fuck, shit, damn, hell, etc.)
~67x

Brandon: Brandon says "10 minutes" as the time cost for literally everything

HALL OF SHAME
Most Repeated Catchphrase
Brandon
Said ~18 times. The DM's version of a verbal tic.
Most Dangerous Habit
Kyle 1
Triggered ~12 SIP-tier rules. If Guidance were a drinking rule, Moor would have killed the table.
Designated Party Tank (most universal triggers caused)
Brandon (DM)
Caused the majority of the 365 universal sip triggers for the whole table
Most Likely to Survive (fewest personal triggers)
Shannon (Opal)
Only 29 personal sips + 10 drinks -- but only because she was blind for 25% of the session
The Lightweight (reached highest status with fewest triggers)
Josh (Bob)
Despite being a stealth god, Josh's sneak attack damage dice consistently rolled 1s and 2s. The universe gave him crits on stealth and took it back on damage.
The DM's Burden (most "Are you sure?" triggers)
Kyle 2 (Aramil)
Between trying to negotiate with hostile gorillas, stealing the golden eye, and casually ending the sun, Brandon questioned Kyle 2's choices more than anyone else at the table.
Best Trigger Moment
Shannon
Raged, nat 1'd, fell into a thermal death beam, took 14 damage to her eyes, went blind for an hour. In two sentences, she created the most quotable moment AND the biggest disaster of the session.
The Enabler
Kelsey
Sent her imp familiar into danger 10+ times while she stayed safely behind. Tika died, was resurrected, and sent right back to work. Girlboss energy.

394 sips49 drinks3 finishes586 oz
Dead
Everyone is dead. Every single person at this table is making death saves IRL. The universal rules alone would have killed you all by the end of hour two. This session was a verbal firehouse of mirrors, damage, swearing, looting, and debate. You are all cautionary tales.*
Bonus Round — Epic Moments
B1"I hate mirrors! Hold this mirror!"
Opal rages, rolls a nat 1, falls into the thermal beam, takes 14 damage, and goes completely blind for an hour. The most poetic self-destruction of the session.
FINISH your drinkWho: Everyone
B2"If the men find out we can shapeshift, they're going to tell the church."
Kelsey delivers the Quote of the Session out of absolutely nowhere during a discussion about turning Tika into a snake. The table erupts.
WATERFALL -- Kelsey starts, everyone follows clockwiseWho: Everyone
B3Aramil steals the golden eye and plunges Faerun into eternal darkness. Kyle 2 rolls an 18 Sleight of Hand as the party is getting in the boat and casually dooms the entire continent for 5,000 gold and a faculty office decoration. "I will put it in my office in the community college."
The person responsible (Kyle 2) FINISHES their drink; everyone else: DRINKWho: Kyle 2 + All
B4Bob's Batman Moment. Josh climbs the wall in total darkness, hangs upside down from the archway, grabs the golden snake idol, and leaps off holding his Cloak of the Bat, gliding to safety like a halfling Bruce Wayne. "I'm a motherfucking knight."
DRINK in reverent silence -- no one speaks for 10 secondsWho: Everyone
B5"And how many squirtons in a gusher?"
The moment Bob asked the real economic question that derailed the first five minutes and established the session's comedic tone. Referenced as gushers, gooshers, and squirts throughout.
SIP every time squirton-adjacent currency is mentioned (came up 6 times)Who: Everyone
B6Opal crits the constrictor snake while blind. Shannon rolls the first and possibly only natural 20 of her night to deal 30 damage and slice through the snake that was squeezing Aramil to death. She can't see. She doesn't care.
Everyone FINISHES their drinkWho: Everyone
Previously On...

Previously On...

One-Shot Recap -- Read aloud at the start of the next session
Performance Notes -- DM Only
This script covers a wild, high-energy one-shot with a comedic core and a dark twist ending. Start warm and slightly absurd -- the opening should feel like a nature documentary narrated by someone who has seen too much. Let the comedy breathe in the early beats, but tighten your delivery as the script accelerates. When you reach the final lines about darkness, slow down dramatically and lower your voice. Look directly at Kyle 2 (Aramil's player) on the last line. Hold the silence for a full five seconds before transitioning.
[MUSIC: Bright, percussive jungle drums layered with exotic bird calls -- adventure-serial energy, almost playful.]
The air is heavy. Not humid -- heavy. Like the jungle itself is leaning on your chest, daring you to breathe. The forbidden shores of Chult stretch before five strangers who answered a flyer posted by a gnome in an oversized cashmere robe -- and not one of them has asked why the pay is so good.
[PAUSE]
A neurotic wizard called Fizzleblast jams a rod into the sand and retreats behind a shimmering barrier he calls the Fizzle Field -- patent pending. He points toward a temple bristling with mirrors that focus sunlight into beams hot enough to melt flesh. Get in. Get the golden snake artifact. Get back before dark. The canoes are rented, and he will not lose his deposit.
His intern Niles trips. A sack of magical items explodes across the beach. A potion of instant attunement shatters. And five mercenaries find themselves bound to whatever touches them first -- armor, bracers, gloves, a pearl -- the expedition's gear chosen not by skill, but by chaos.
[MUSIC: The drums deepen. Strings creep in underneath -- tension building beneath the adventure.]
Inside the temple, the antechamber gleams with mountains of gold and the bones of those who grabbed too greedily. Pressure plates. Poison gas. A massive stone door held by rotting ropes. A mural on the wall shows a man and a crocodile walking arm in arm, swords crossed. It is a riddle. It is a warning. A dragonborn cleric and a tiefling warlock link arms, step in lockstep, and the temple lets them pass.
[PAUSE]
Then the crystalline chamber. Mirrors splitting light into blinding beams. A giant constrictor snake, forty feet of coiled muscle and venom, guarding a prism at the heart of the room. A goliath with a great axe swings blind -- literally blind, her eyes seared by a misdirected beam -- and still she buries the blade. The snake falls. The path opens.
[MUSIC: Sharp, driving percussion. The pace doubles. No more safety.]
Beyond the inner gate, open sky. A canyon. And sleeping beneath a stone arch, bathed in mirror-light like a basking god -- a tyrant lizard. A halfling rogue in a bat-wing cloak scales the wall in total darkness. Silent hands. The golden idol lifts free. He leaps.
A fireball screams down from above. Rivals. The T-Rex wakes.
[PAUSE]
Four minutes. A skill challenge at a dead sprint through collapsing corridors, reactivated blade traps, and a stone door that takes every ounce of strength to raise. Two mages materialize in the doorway. A cleric commands one to flee. A warlock fills the other's mind with phantom raptors. They run.
The jungle rises. The dead claw out of the earth. The rivals scream. The bubble holds.
[MUSIC: The music cuts. Total silence. Then -- a single, low, sustained tone. Something wrong.]
Morning. The party sits safe inside the Fizzle Field, loot heavy in their packs. But a half-elf with a stained fedora and the desperate eyes of a disgraced professor reaches into the golden idol when no one is looking. His fingers close around a jeweled eye. He pockets it. No one sees.
[PAUSE]
And the sun does not rise.
[MUSIC: The low tone fades to nothing. Absolute silence.]
Quick Reference
Player-Specific Hooks
Moor: ** A cleric of Tyr who kept everyone alive through 148 HP of healing -- now the sun his god watches over has gone dark
Aramil: ** Possesses the stolen Eye of the Serpent and carries a secret that may have doomed the continent -- all for his father's approval
Opal: ** Fought blind and still killed the snake, but the Belt of Hill Giant Strength and a Bloodaxe cannot fix a world without light
Lavender: ** Session MVP whose imp Tika and quick thinking saved the party repeatedly -- but her promised deal to free her genie patron Jezebel remains unfulfilled
Bob: ** Carries the golden idol itself, the Cloak of the Bat, and most of the loot -- a thief who thrives in darkness, and darkness is all that remains
What Comes Next

Follow-Up Scenarios

Post-Mortem + 3 Alternate Universe Hooks
Session Post-Mortem
What worked, what needs work, and where the story goes next.
OK
Key Successes
4
01
Puzzle Design:
The synchronized pressure plate crossing and mirror-beam prism room gave every player a moment to contribute mechanically. Moor's "why use many word" moment was born directly from puzzle engagement.
02
Emergent Comedy:
The currency debate ("how many squirtons in a gusher?"), Opal's blind snake kill, and the Niles loot explosion provided organic levity that never undercut the tension.
03
The Gut Punch Ending:
Aramil's secret theft and the sun going out transformed a lighthearted dungeon crawl into a world-altering catastrophe. The tonal whiplash was earned because the murals foreshadowed it.
04
Loot Distribution:
The Potion of Instant Attunement mechanic let every PC feel powerful without the usual loot negotiation. Bob's Cloak of the Bat and Lavender's fire immunity both paid off dramatically during the escape.
!!
Areas to Address
4
!!
The Aramil Problem:
Kyle 2 knows what Aramil did. The other players do not. A sequel session needs a clear plan for when and how the eye theft is revealed -- player-driven discovery or DM-forced confrontation.
!!
Tone Calibration:
The one-shot was comedic adventure with a horror stinger. A sequel ("Tube of Annihilation") would need to decide: do we lean into the darkness, or keep the comedy and treat eternal night as a backdrop?
!!
Opal's Blindness:
Moor healed it this time, but the mechanic of Opal losing senses could become a recurring gag or a genuine character vulnerability. Decide which.
!!
Stakes Escalation:
The sun is gone. That is an extinction-level event. A sequel needs to either make reversing it the central quest or establish why the world hasn't immediately collapsed.
>>
Campaign Hooks
3
1
The Return Trip:
Fizzleblast demands the party return to the temple to replace the eye. He knows more than he let on, and now the T-Rex is awake.
2
Aramil's Reckoning:
The party discovers the theft. Aramil must choose between his father's approval and saving the world.
3
The Eagle Rider:
The only other temple survivor appears with critical information -- and a grudge.
Alternate Universe One-Shots
Divergence points from the session — each a fully playable premise where the story took a different path. Click to expand.
Click a scenario node to scroll to its card below
A
SCENARIO A
The Eye Stays
What If Aramil Didn't Steal the Eye?
HEISTTHRILLERMORAL
2-3 sessions. Infiltrate Fizzleblast's lab, steal the idol back, return to the temple, replace the idol before the last city goes dark.
▼
The Premise
Aramil sees the murals, understands the warning, and for once in his miserable academic career, makes the right call. He leaves the Eye of the Serpent in the idol. The sun rises. The undead stay dead. Fizzleblast gets his artifact intact. Everyone gets paid.
The Twist
Without the eternal darkness as a wake-up call, nobody realizes the idol was a containment device. Fizzleblast takes it back to his workshop and begins "studying" it -- which means disassembling it. Three weeks later, the containment fails anyway, but this time there is no Eye to restore. The darkness falls slowly, city by city, as the idol's power drains. And Aramil, haunted by the knowledge that he COULD have taken the eye and prevented Fizzleblast's tampering, must now steal it from a fortified gnomish laboratory to save the world he almost doomed.
Tone
Heist thriller with moral complexity. Ocean's Eleven meets Indiana Jones. Aramil has to become the thief he chose not to be.
B
SCENARIO B
The King Wakes
What If the T-Rex Wasn't Sleeping?
HORRORCOMEDYTENSENEGOTIATIONDARK
1 session. A single extended social encounter with combat as the fail state. Every wrong word brings the jaws closer.
▼
The Premise
The rival party's fireball doesn't wake the T-Rex. The T-Rex was never asleep. It was waiting. The canyon is its hunting ground, and the idol is the bait. Every expedition Fizzleblast sends is food.
The Twist
When Bob reaches for the idol, the T-Rex opens one eye. It has been watching. It is intelligent -- not beast-intelligent, but cursed-king intelligent. It was the temple's last guardian, polymorphed and bound centuries ago, and it speaks in a voice like grinding stone: "Another thief. How many must I eat before they stop coming?" The party must negotiate with a tyrannosaur who is also a furious, immortal Chultan king, convince it they are not like the others, and somehow retrieve the idol without becoming lunch.
Tone
Tense negotiation horror with dark comedy. Jurassic Park meets a hostage situation. The T-Rex is the smartest person in the room and it is very, very tired of adventurers.
C
SCENARIO C
The Patron's Price
What If Fizzleblast Was the Villain?
HORRORBETRAYAL
1-2 sessions. The betrayal reveal, the boss fight, and a desperate escape into an undead-infested jungle with no safety net.
▼
The Premise
Fizzleblast is not a bumbling gnome academic. He is a warlock of Vecna who has spent decades engineering the removal of the idol to plunge Faerun into eternal darkness. Every "failed" expedition was a test run. Every dead adventurer was a sacrifice. The "accidental" public posting, the rival party, the loot explosion -- all orchestrated. And the Fizzle Field is not a safety bubble. It is a ritual circle.
The Twist
The party escapes the temple with the idol, crosses into the Fizzle Field, and the barrier seals behind them. Fizzleblast is not asleep. He is standing in the center of a glowing ritual circle, spectacles gone, cashmere robe replaced by vestments of Vecna, Niles bound and gagged at his feet. "Thank you for bringing it to me," he says. "You may leave your weapons with the intern." The party must fight their patron, inside his own prepared magical fortress, with no escape route, while the undead claw at the barrier from outside.
Tone
Horror betrayal. The friendly quest-giver was the final boss. Everything they trusted was a lie. Think Cabin in the Woods -- the system was designed to kill them, and they walked right into it.
DM Report Card

DM Report Card

Session Quality Assessment -- How well the DM's decisions, design, and storytelling served the table
A+
8.7/10
Overall Session Engagement
This session hit its highest engagement during the T-Rex heist and the final escape skill challenge, where every player was laser-focused and the stakes were palpable. The engagement floor was during the inventory management and loot distribution phases, which is entirely normal — and even those moments were punctuated by jokes and roleplay ("I'm a fan of big ladies. Big ladies are fans of me."). For a one-shot with players who hadn't played together recently ("It's been so long since we played"), this level of investment is remarkable.
~4.5 hours
Session duration (estimated)
30+
Laughter instances
~35-40% of total dialogueDM words spoken
3Unique NPCs voiced
3Combat encounters
4+Roleplay scenes
5Puzzle/exploration sequences
3Plot hooks planted
8-10Improvised moments (estimated)
15+Player-initiated actions responded to
6"Oh shit" moments
Engagement Breakdown
9
Player Responsiveness
Players jumped on every prompt with zero prodding. When Brandon described the gorallons approaching, Moor immediately said "I'd like to see if the mirror can be turned" — no hesitation, pure tactical instinct. When the loot bag exploded, players were rolling initiative before being asked.
9
Emotional Investment
Opal screaming "I hate mirrors! Hold this mirror!" while raging. Aramil desperately trying to talk down the gorilla — "Think about your wife and kids." Lavender refusing to abandon her genie's wishes. The collective panic when Aramil dropped to -10 HP from the constrictor snake.
8
Voluntary Engagement
Players constantly took unprompted actions: Bob volunteered to be a decoy for the baboons ("I'm going to start screaming and making as much noise as I can"), Aramil independently investigated the murals, Lavender proactively sent Tika to spy on the rival team. Moor cast Light on a rock under his shirt — "waiting" for the right moment.
9
Creative Participation
The mirror-redirect puzzle solutions were entirely player-driven. Bob's spider-slipper ceiling escape was genius. Lavender using Gust of Wind to clear poison gas was clutch improvisation. The Indiana Jones-style scale swap was a perfect collaborative moment between Bob and Moor.
9
Table Energy
The squirtons/gushers currency bit had the entire table crying within the first five minutes. "If the men find out we can shapeshift, they're going to tell the church" became an instant classic. Cross-talk was constant, positive, and building on each other's jokes.
8
Session Completion Satisfaction
"Good job, Brandon." "That was fun." "That was great." "That was really fun." "I can't believe we did it." — Multiple players expressing genuine satisfaction. Post-session, they immediately wanted to know about other groups' attempts and debated what they could have done differently. Aramil said "Seems like we need to play that next" about Tomb of Annihilation.
What Your Players Loved Most
01
The Real-Time Escape Skill Challenge
Skill Challenge / Chase Sequence
2 Details▼
Brandon ran a timed escape sequence with a real-world timer — 4 minutes and 20 seconds to reach Fizzleblast's safety bubble, with rotating skill checks, a rival team ambush via Dimension Door, and the entire Chultian jungle collapsing into undead chaos behind them.
Why It Worked
The real-time pressure transformed a group of chatty, deliberative players ("That's not our style") into a decisive unit. Every roll mattered. The two failures (13 damage each from blade traps) raised genuine fear — "One more and somebody dies." The rival team's Dimension Door ambush right at the finish line was a perfect final twist that let Moor's Command spell and Aramil's Phantasmal Force shin
DM Skill
Pacing mastery and tension architecture. Brandon knew exactly when to introduce the timer — not at the start, but at the moment of commitment. "My rule is when you start the skill challenge, that's when I tell you how much time you have left."
02
Bob's Batman Heist on the T-Rex Archway
Stealth / Heist Encounter
2 Details▼
Brandon designed the idol retrieval as a multi-layered puzzle — a sleeping T-Rex directly beneath a hanging golden snake idol, pterodactyls that would screech and wake it, rival adventurers on the roof planning to break mirrors and weaponize the T-Rex, and total darkness closing in.
Why It Worked
It rewarded Bob's entire character build — the spider slippers, the Cloak of the Bat, the thief abilities. Every item Brandon had distributed earlier paid off in this single moment. The fireball from the rival team hitting the T-Rex instead of killing Bob was cinematic perfection.
DM Skill
Encounter design that rewards player investment. The T-Rex encounter wasn't a combat — it was an Ocean's Eleven heist that made the rogue feel like the most important person in the world.
03
Opal's Blind Snake Kill (Critical Hit)
Combat Encounter / Character Moment
2 Details▼
After going blind from a natural 1 on the mirror push (14 radiant damage to her eyes), Opal spent nearly an hour of game time unable to see. When the giant constrictor snake grappled Aramil and squeezed him to -10 HP, Opal — still narratively recovering her vision — rolled a critical hit with her great axe for 30 damage, cleaving through the coiled layers of the snake to kill it.
Why It Worked
The blindness wasn't just a debuff — it was a character arc. Lavender gave Opal a bandana to cover her eyes. Moor healed her. When she finally struck, it felt earned. The crit was pure luck, but the emotional setup was all Brandon's encounter design.
DM Skill
Consequence design. The nat 1 blindness could have been punitive; instead, Brandon made it a story beat with a timer, a medical check, and a cure available through the Staff of Healing he'd planted in the loot.
04
The Squirtons Currency Debate
05
Fizzleblast's Introduction and the Loot Bag Explosion
NPC Spotlight
F
Favorite NPC
Fizzleblast
Most Invested
✓ Referenced by name throughout the session by all five players
✓ Players adopted his mannerisms — Opal echoed "the cashmere is important," Lavender played along with the hat-on-horns bi
✓ Questions asked about Fizzleblast: his relationship with Niles, his knowledge of the artifact, his safety bubble mechani
✓ Emotional reactions: laughter at every Fizzleblast line, affectionate mockery of his incompetence
✓ "One of us doesn't have eyes." — Opal, during Fizzleblast's roll call
✓ "There's so much cashmere in this jungle!" — Lavender
✓ "Well, that makes sense." — Opal, upon learning Fizzleblast accidentally posted to public forums
✓
Why This NPC Worked
Fizzleblast was a perfectly calibrated quest-giver — competent enough to have found the temple and built a safety field, but eccentric enough to post his plans on "mercenaries, murders, and mothers-in-law's public forums" instead of the private channel. He was funny without being a joke. The cashmere obsession, the Niles abuse, the "Touch nothing but the lamp — wrong franchise" bit — these were specific character details that made him feel real. Most importantly, he had teeth: "Not without the idol" when they tried to enter the safety bubble. He wasn't a pushover; he was a lovable bastard.
N
Runner-Up NPC
Niles — the long-suffering intern whose fumble with the loot sack drove the entire attunement mechanic. "I remember being a graduate assistant" resonated because we've all been Niles.
Most Tense Moment
Peak Tension
The Constrictor Snake Grapples Aramil to -10 HP
Duration
Approximately 15 minutes of real time from the snake's emergence to its death, with the peak lasting about 3-4 minutes from Aramil's downing to Opal's crit.
What Was At Stake
Aramil's life. At -10 HP, one more failed death save or a single point of constriction damage would have meant permanent character death. The snake was still alive, still squeezing, and the party was also dealing with rising heat, dwindling resources, and creatures dropping into the room from above.
How You Built It
Brandon layered threats expertly. The snake emerged from the heated statue eye — an environmental consequence, not a random encounter. It had multi-attack (bite + constrict), paralytic venom that took out Tika, and pack intelligence that made it target the squishiest party member. When it grappled Aramil with an 18 against his 5, the math was devastating: 22 damage when Aramil only had 12 HP. Brandon didn't fudge it. He let the dice speak.
✓
Resolution
Opal's critical hit with the great axe for 30 damage — "I'm going to chop like a cake, but less delicately" — killed the snake in one strike. Moor immediately Cure Wounds'd Aramil back to 15 HP. The relief was palpable.
DM Technique
"Escalating consequences" — The snake wasn't just damage; it was grapple into squeeze into unconsciousness, each stage worse than the last, with the party's resources already depleted from the gas trap (14 damage to everyone) and the heat mechanic ticking in the background.

9/10
Player Agency Score
Fairness Assessment
Meaningful Choices
Strong
The pressure plate puzzle had multiple valid solutions (paired walking, gold weighting, jumping). The T-Rex encounter offered stealth, diplomacy (Speak with Animals), combat, and misdirection as viable paths. The door rope mechanic gave real tactical weight to time management.
Consequence Follow-through
Strong
Opal's nat 1 on the mirror resulted in blindness — real, lasting, with a timer and a cure path. Aramil's failed persuasion of the snake led directly to being targeted. Lavender removing the Zephyr Reliquary caused the room to heat up. Every action had a ripple.
Combat Balance
Challenging but fair
The constrictor snake nearly killed Aramil (to -10 HP), the gas trap hit the entire party for 14 damage each, and the baboon swarm created genuine pressure. But healing resources (Staff of Healing, Moor's cleric kit) were sufficient to recover. No fight felt unwinnable.
Information Availability
Generous
Brandon consistently gave information through multiple channels: visual descriptions, investigation checks with low DCs for time-invested players, murals that hinted at consequences, and even the genie's wind calling Lavender to the reliquary. The mural warning about removing the serpent eye was available — Aramil just chose to ignore it.
Spotlight Distribution
Slightly uneven (see below)
Every player had a defining moment, though Lavender and Bob received slightly more mechanical spotlight due to their builds being well-suited to the encounter types.
Rule Consistency
Mostly consistent
Brandon bent rules warmly for the one-shot format — automatic attunement via the potion, simplified encumbrance, generous "take 10 minutes" options. One acknowledged fudge (Shannon's roll when she was away). Always in the players' favor, always acknowledged.
Your DM Strengths This Session
{}Environmental Encounter Design
The ability to create encounters where the environment is as much a character as the monsters — where terrain, mechanics, and hazards interlock to create emergent gameplay.
~~Loot as Narrative Architecture
Using item distribution not just as rewards but as setup for future encounters — every item has a payoff scene.
<>Real-Time Pressure Mechanics
**What we saw:** The 10-minute sequence timer and the final 4:20 escape timer transformed this group's natural tendency toward deliberation into a source of tension. "That's not our style" became "We gotta go." **Why it matters:** This group loves to talk. That's their joy. But you found the exact r
DM Archetype
The Tactician-Director Blend
You built encounters that demanded creative problem-solving (Tactician) and then presented them with cinematic flair — the T-Rex under a heat lamp, the fireball hitting the archway "Jurassic Park-style," the undead erupting from the ground as the sun died (Director). Your descriptions were always in service of gameplay, not just atmosphere. Every detail you narrated had mechanical relevance. The cobwebs told them ceiling height. The rope condition told them fragility. The pressure plates had obs
Sequel Hooks & Unresolved Threads
Eternal Darkness / Sun Extinguished
Planted and detonated
Players fully aware — Aramil stole the golden eye, causing the sun to go out across Faerun
Direct setup for Tomb of Annihilation campaign; the party is personally responsible for the apocalypse
Aramil's Father's Approval
Developing
Aramil mentioned it openly ("Maybe my dad finally noticed me")
Character arc for a full campaign — does the discovery earn his father's respect, or does the apocalypse he caused destroy any chance?
The Rival Adventuring Party (survivors)
Planted
Players know three of six rivals survived and saw them
Recurring antagonists; they know the party stole the idol and may blame them for the darkness
Fizzleblast's Reaction to the Eye Theft
Planted
Fizzleblast was asleep during the theft; he doesn't know yet
Potential betrayal reveal — Fizzleblast hired them and was cheated by Aramil
Unexplored Temple Sections
Planted
Players explicitly noted "We did not explore the entirety of the dungeon. Not even close."
Return expedition, now in eternal darkness without the mirror defense system
Niles's Trauma
Planted
"Niles is on the floor just shaking. He's seen something"
What did Niles witness? A horror hook hiding in plain sight
Opal's Obsidian Rock (unclaimed)
Developing
Opal wanted it but left it behind ("My obsidian rock. Don't let me forget.")
Unfinished business pulling the party back to the temple
Strongest Hook
The eternal darkness. Aramil's player literally said "Seems like we need to play that next." The hook is not just planted — the player is watering it.
Sleeper Thread
Niles. "He's seen something, and you don't know what." Nobody asked follow-up questions. That's a time bomb.
Personalized Message
Brandon,

The moment that defined this session for me was the T-Rex. Not the creature itself — the architecture of the encounter. A sleeping apex predator bathing under mirror-focused sunlight, a golden idol hanging from an archway directly above its head, pterodactyls that would screech at anything unfamiliar, and a rival team on the roof planning to break the mirrors and weaponize the dinosaur against your players. That is five independent threat vectors operating simultaneously, and not one of them required a single attack roll to navigate. You built a heist, not a fight, and your players rose to it magnificently.

What nobody else would have done the same way: the loot. Every single item you distributed in that chaotic bag-explosion scene had a specific payoff encounter designed for it later in the dungeon. The Cloak of the Bat. The Zephyr Reliquary. The Boots of Striding. The Staff of Healing that cured the blindness that came from the mirrors that powered the puzzle that guarded the snake that protected the idol. That chain of causation is not accident — that is preparation, and it is exceptional.

The risky part — and you know this — was the timer. This group loves to deliberate. They spent ten minutes debating how many squirtons fit in a gusher. Putting a real-time clock on them could have felt punitive. Instead, it felt like the most exciting four minutes and twenty seconds of the entire session. "That's not our style" became their style, because you earned their trust for three hours before you tested it.

You ran this for other groups before. Some teams had twenty minutes and still failed the skill challenge. Your players had four and a half and made it — the first full party to ever survive. They know that. They're proud of it. And Aramil is already asking about Tomb of Annihilation.

The sun went out in Faerun because your players had so much fun they didn't want to leave anything behind. That is the highest compliment a dungeon can receive.

*Report generated from session transcript analysis. All data points derived from observable player behavior and dialogue. This report is for the DM's eyes only — unless they want to share it.*

World Wiki

The Verdant Codex

31 Lore Entries --- Threats, Locations, Factions, Items & Quests
Table of Contents
THREAT3

THREAT ASSESSMENT: The Rival Party (Blackwater)
THREAT
ThreatHighStatusUnknown (Presumed Dead)AffiliationUnknown Patron / BuyerFirst SeenSession 1 (mentioned by Fizzleblast)AKARed Fangs, Black Skulls, Something Edgy
Classification: High | Status: Unknown (Presumed Dead) | Last Known Activity: Session 1
“They'll definitely kill you guys.” -- Fizzleblast, with characteristic understatement
Executive Summary

The Rival Party -- referred to by Fizzleblast as “Red Fangs, Black Skulls, something edgy” -- was a competing adventuring team that followed Charisma Company to the Chultan temple after Fizzleblast accidentally posted expedition details on a public forum. They arrived approximately one hour behind the party and positioned themselves on the temple's upper ridgeline to observe and wait. Their strategy was classic vulture tactics: let the party do the dangerous work of clearing traps and solving puzzles, then steal the artifact during extraction. [CONFIRMED]

The rival party included at least two spellcasters capable of Fireball and Dimension Door -- significant magical resources for a temple heist. They launched a Fireball at Bob as he flew from the archway with the Golden Snake Idol, narrowly missing him but waking the Tyrannosaur and triggering the climactic escape. Two members appeared via Dimension Door between the party and the Fizzle Field safety perimeter, but were routed by Aramil's Phantasmal Force (the target rolled a natural 1 on the Intelligence save). [CONFIRMED]

The critical unknown: their ultimate fate. They fled into the Chultan jungle at night, moments before the undead rose and the darkness became permanent. Screams were heard. Whether any survived is undetermined. [UNKNOWN]

Known Abilities
AbilityTypeObserved EffectThreat LevelSessionNotes
FireballOffensive MagicLaunched from ridgeline; hit the archway and the T-Rex. Bob took 9 half damage (Dex save 24). Full blast woke the Tyrannosaur.CRITICAL1At least one caster with 3rd-level slots.
Dimension DoorMovementTwo members appeared between the party and the safety bubble -- teleported from the ridgeline to the beach.HIGH1At least one caster with 4th-level slots or scrolls.
Tactical PositioningStrategicPositioned on the ridgeline above the temple, observing the party's progress without engaging. Waited for the optimal ambush moment.HIGH1Disciplined and patient -- not impulsive.
Tactical Profile
Fighting StyleVulture tactics. They did not engage the temple's traps or inhabitants. They waited for the party to clear the path, then struck during extraction. This is a team built for ambush, not dungeon crawling. [CONFIRMED]
OpeningLong-range magical strike (Fireball) from an elevated, concealed position. [CONFIRMED]
When LosingFled into the jungle when Phantasmal Force broke their nerve. The target rolled a Nat 1 on the save and panicked, grabbing his companion and running. [CONFIRMED]
Estimated Power LevelComparable to or slightly above the party. Multiple 3rd- and 4th-level casters suggest a well-funded, professional operation. [ASSESSED]
Known Weaknesses
WeaknessEvidenceConfidence
Morale collapse under magical fearOne member fled from Phantasmal Force (Nat 1 save), dragging an ally with himHIGH
Dependent on the party's successTheir entire strategy required the party to solve the temple firstCONFIRMED
Intelligence Gaps
☐Total team size -- only two members were directly encountered
☐Identity of their employer/buyer
☐Whether any survived the undead rising in the jungle
☐Their original base of operations and organizational affiliation
☐Whether they had additional teams or resources in reserve
THREAT ASSESSMENT: The Giant Constrictor Snake
THREAT
ThreatHighStatusDeadAffiliationTemple GuardianFirst SeenSession 1AKAThe Temple Serpent, Brother Snake
Classification: High | Status: Dead | Last Known Activity: Session 1
Executive Summary

The Giant Constrictor Snake served as the temple's primary biological guardian, emerging from one of the stone serpent statues in the Prism Room when the heat from the redirected mirror beams roused it from hibernation. Approximately forty feet long with a body thick as a man's torso, the snake was an apex predator that had survived in the temple's sealed environment for an unknown period. [CONFIRMED]

It was the most dangerous single combat encounter of the session. The snake downed Aramil to -10 HP in a single constriction (22 damage) and killed Tika (Lavender's imp familiar) before being killed by Opal -- who was blind at the time -- with a critical hit greataxe strike for 30 damage. [CONFIRMED]

Known Abilities
AbilityTypeObserved EffectThreat LevelSessionNotes
ConstrictGrapple + Damage22 damage in a single round; grappled and restrained Aramil, dropping him from conscious to -10 HPCRITICAL1Automatic damage each round while grappled
BiteMelee AttackCaught Tika (imp familiar) in its jaws, killing the familiarHIGH1Multi-attack with bite + constrict
Speed/ReachMovementLunged past Opal's guard to reach Aramil in the back lineMODERATE1Faster than its bulk suggested
Weaknesses Exploited
WeaknessEvidenceConfidence
Vulnerable to slashing damageOpal's greataxe critical hit (30 damage) killed it; blade “sheared through one loop, then another”HIGH
No ranged defenseSusceptible to Eldritch Blast and shortbow fire from rangeCONFIRMED
Not immune to Speak with AnimalsAramil communicated with it, though persuasion failedCONFIRMED
THREAT ASSESSMENT: Ransom's Rex (The Tyrannosaur)
THREAT
ThreatExtremeStatusActive (Awake and Angry)AffiliationNone -- Apex PredatorFirst SeenSession 1AKAThe T-Rex, The Tyrant King
Classification: Extreme | Status: Active | Last Known Activity: Session 1
Executive Summary

Ransom's Rex is a fully grown Tyrannosaur that was sleeping in the temple's open-air canyon courtyard, bathing in the warmth of the mirror arrays. The party did NOT fight it -- they successfully avoided combat through stealth, but the rival party's Fireball woke it, triggering the escape sequence. [CONFIRMED]

Aramil read the dinosaur's surface thoughts via the Helm of Telepathy: it was “annoyed with the pterodactyls when they screech” -- suggesting a creature of limited cognition but clear territorial instincts. [CONFIRMED] Every other predator in the canyon -- crocodiles, boa constrictors, gorallons -- refused to descend while the Tyrannosaur was present, indicating absolute apex dominance. [CONFIRMED]

The party survived by never engaging. If this encounter continues, the Tyrannosaur represents a CRITICAL threat that the party cannot defeat at Level 3.

Port Nyanzaru
LOCATION
StatusIntact (Pre-Darkness)FactionMerchant PrincesTypeCityRegionChultFirst VisitedSession 1 (referenced)AKANyanzaru
A city of dinosaur races, merchant princes, and the persistent smell of jungle rot and exotic spices -- now plunged into a darkness that may never lift.
Overview

Port Nyanzaru is the primary civilized settlement on the Chultan peninsula and the staging ground for Fizzleblast's expedition. The party departed from its marina in rented canoes and would presumably return there after the mission. [CONFIRMED] The city is governed by Merchant Princes who control trade, resources, and policy. [EXTRAPOLATED from D&D canon]

The city features a Grand Souk marketplace, temples to multiple deities (including Tyr, Savras, Waukeen, and Ubtao), a harbor district, and a marina where Fizzleblast rented his canoes (Slip 7). [CONFIRMED -- Fizzleblast's logistical details; EXTRAPOLATED for broader features]

Current Status

Following Aramil's theft of the Eye of the Serpent, Port Nyanzaru is now in crisis. The sun has not risen. The undead are rising across the peninsula. Candle and oil prices have tripled. The temples are overwhelmed. The Flaming Fist garrison at Fort Beluarian has gone silent. [EXTRAPOLATED from session consequences]

The Shore / Fizzle Field Landing Site
LOCATION
StatusIntactFactionCharisma Company (temporary)TypeLandmarkRegionChultan JungleFirst VisitedSession 1AKALanding Site, Fizzle Field, The Beach
Black sand, screaming jungle, and a shimmering protective bubble where a gnome slept through the apocalypse.
Vivid Description

The landing site is a strip of black volcanic sand where the river meets the Chultan jungle interior. The canoes scraped against the shore as the party arrived, and the jungle screamed -- howler monkeys, cicadas, unseen things with too many limbs. The air carried weight: thick with humidity, rot, scale, and earth turned over by massive feet. [CONFIRMED -- DM narration]

The Fizzle Field is Fizzleblast's patented protective bubble -- a shimmering energy barrier that repels “organic matter, undead matter, and aggressive sales tactics.” [CANONICAL] It is translucent, allowing those inside to see out (Niles witnessed something traumatic through the barrier during the night). The bubble served as the party's extraction point and sole safe haven. [CONFIRMED]

Notable Features
FeatureDescriptionSignificance
Fizzle Field BubbleShimmering protective barrier; patent pendingOnly safe zone; undead could not penetrate; sound is muted inside
Black Sand BeachVolcanic sand shorelineCanoe landing point; loot explosion occurred here
Canoes (x2)Rented from Port Nyanzaru Marina24-hour rental; must be returned by noon; Fizzleblast's deposit at risk
Events
SessionEventParticipantsConsequences
1Arrival and briefingFull party, Fizzleblast, NilesMission established; equipment distributed via Loot Explosion
1Loot ExplosionNiles (catalyst), full partyMagical items randomly bound to each character via Potion of Instant Attunement
1Final escape / extractionFull partyParty reached the bubble; undead rose outside; rival party screams heard
1Dawn fails to arriveAllThe sun does not rise; sky remains black; Niles found shaking on the floor
Temple of the Man and Crocodile
LOCATION
StatusIntact (traps partially deactivated)FactionNone (abandoned)TypeDungeonRegionChultan JungleFirst VisitedSession 1AKAThe Chultan Temple, The Snake Temple
A solar-powered death trap built by a civilization that understood one truth: the world runs on sunlight, and the serpent holds the key.
Vivid Description

The temple is an ancient Chultan structure built around a central engineering principle: heliographic thermal arrays (mirrors) that focus sunlight into directed beams capable of melting flesh. The entire complex is a solar clock -- the beams only align at specific times of day, and the mechanisms depend entirely on sunlight to function. [CONFIRMED]

The architecture combines stone construction with advanced optical engineering. Mirrors mounted on adjustable stone housings redirect sunlight through corridors, into a central crystalline prism, and ultimately to four gems above the inner portcullis. The design philosophy is “cooperation over conquest” -- the pressure plates, the paired sword mechanism, and the Man-and-Crocodile mural all require two people working in synchronization. [CONFIRMED]

Sub-Location Details
Temple Entrance / Heliographic Array

The entrance is flanked by heliographic thermal arrays -- mirrors angled to focus sunlight into lethal beams. Two Gorallons (four-armed jungle apes) paced at the edge of the light, their skin blistering where the beams caught them. Moor turned the first mirror to melt a Gorallon; Aramil intimidated the second into fleeing. [CONFIRMED]

Antechamber of Blades

A rectangular chamber thirty feet high, ceiling lost in shadow and cobwebs. Treasure heaped along the walls -- gold coins, weapons, trinkets, magical artifacts, interspersed with skeletons. Two rows of pressure plates run the length of the chamber, with residual blood spray marking where previous adventurers failed. A mural at the far end depicts a man and crocodile walking in lockstep with crossed swords overhead -- the key to the pressure plate puzzle. Two rusted greatswords hang above the main gate as the final activation mechanism. [CONFIRMED]

Prism Room / Crystalline Heart

The temple's mechanical core. A massive crystalline prism hangs from a central bar, splitting focused beams into spectral lances. Two enormous stone snake statues serve as reflectors. Four unlit gems crown the far portcullis -- all four must be lit simultaneously to open the way. The Giant Constrictor Snake emerged from one of the stone snake statues. Poison gas vents line the floor, triggered by a dead baboon falling on a pressure plate. [CONFIRMED]

Gas Chamber / Monkey Room

A section of the temple infested with feral baboons and accessible from the upper terrace. Noxious gas traps deal 14 poison damage to all creatures in the area. The baboons were drawn by the temple's rising heat after Lavender removed the Zephyr Reliquary (the temple's cooling mechanism). [CONFIRMED]

Snake Sanctum

The inner chambers containing treasure hoards, skeleton guardians (9 skeletons, 7 destroyed by Moor's Turn Undead), and the mechanisms for redirecting the snake statue reflectors. Additional loot found here included the Staff of Healing, Belt of Hill Giant Strength, Bloodaxe, and Periapt of Wound Closure. [CONFIRMED]

T-Rex Courtyard

An open-air canyon at the temple's crown, enclosed by ridged stone walls. The jungle presses in from above -- crocodiles on the ridgeline, boa constrictors draped over ledges, gorallons watching. None descend because the Tyrannosaur sleeps in the center, bathing in the last concentrated warmth of the mirror arrays. The Golden Snake Idol hangs from an archway above the sleeping Rex. [CONFIRMED]

Atmosphere
Hot stone. The taste of old copper and dry blood. Focused sunlight that burns rather than warms. The sound of mechanisms grinding in the walls -- gears that have been turning for centuries. A silence in the deepest chambers that is not emptiness but patience.
Charisma Company
FACTION
StatusActiveDispositionEmployer (Transactional)TypeMercantile / ExpeditionaryFirst MentionedSession 1AKACC
“We Go Where You Won't!”
Overview

Charisma Company is Fizzleblast's expeditionary services outfit, specializing in artifact retrieval through the employment of adventurers willing to accept risk the proprietor will not. The company's model is simple: Fizzleblast identifies targets through research, hires expendable teams, provides magical equipment as a “signing bonus,” and remains safely behind a protective barrier while his contractors do the dangerous work. [CONFIRMED]

The company operates out of Port Nyanzaru (Slip 7 at the marina) and recruits through both private channels and -- accidentally -- public forums such as “Mercenaries, Murders, and Mothers-in-Law.” [CONFIRMED] Fizzleblast is the sole executive. Niles is the sole employee (intern/graduate assistant). The company's track record is poor: “my last three teams did not” survive. [CONFIRMED]

Leadership
NameTitle/RoleStatus
FizzleblastProprietor, WizardActive
NilesIntern / Graduate AssistantActive (Traumatized)
Resources
Magical EquipmentFizzleblast provided a Bag of Holding containing multiple magical items and a Potion of Instant Attunement. The total value of distributed equipment significantly exceeded the 500gp retrieval fee. [CONFIRMED]
Fizzle Field TechnologyFizzleblast's patented protective barrier is his most significant asset -- a portable safe zone that repels organic matter, undead, and hostile entities. [CONFIRMED]
Financial ResourcesSufficient to rent canoes, outfit an expedition, and distribute magical equipment. Not extravagant -- he is concerned about canoe deposits. [CONFIRMED]
IntelligenceFizzleblast identified the temple, researched its defenses (heliographic arrays), and determined the artifact's general location. His intelligence was incomplete but actionable. [CONFIRMED]
Relationship with Party
PCStandingBasis
MoorNeutralProfessional. Answered roll call, completed mission.
OpalAmused toleranceFizzleblast wrote “Oaf” instead of “Opal.”
AramilHostile (if discovered)Aramil stole the Eye of the Serpent from Fizzleblast's artifact.
LavenderWarmLet him hang his hat on her horns. Complimentary about cashmere.
BobTransactionalDelivered the idol. Minimal personal interaction.
Open Questions
☐Is Charisma Company a legitimate business or a front for something else?
☐Has Fizzleblast noticed the missing Eye from the idol?
☐What does he intend to do with the Golden Snake Idol?
☐How will he respond to the eternal darkness -- with investigation, panic, or profit?
☐Were there previous Charisma Company expeditions, and what happened to those teams?
The Blackwater Rivals
FACTION
StatusUnknown (Presumed Destroyed)DispositionHostileTypeMercenary / CriminalFirst MentionedSession 1AKARed Fangs, Black Skulls, Something Edgy
“They'll definitely kill you guys.” -- Fizzleblast
Overview

A rival adventuring party hired by an unknown buyer to retrieve the same artifact Charisma Company was targeting. They followed the party to the temple, positioned themselves on the ridgeline, and attempted to steal the Golden Snake Idol through ambush during the party's extraction. Their attack (Fireball + Dimension Door positioning) was sophisticated but ultimately failed when Aramil's Phantasmal Force broke their morale. [CONFIRMED]

They fled into the jungle at night, moments before the undead rose. Screams were heard. Their fate is unknown. [CONFIRMED]

Resources
Magical CapabilityAt least two spellcasters with 3rd-level (Fireball) and 4th-level (Dimension Door) slots. This represents significant arcane investment. [CONFIRMED]
IntelligenceThey knew about the expedition, the temple location, and the artifact. Their orders demonstrated familiarity with the temple's mirror defenses. [ASSESSED]
Unknown PatronSomeone hired and equipped them. That patron has resources, knowledge, and intent that remain unaccounted for. [ASSESSED]
Church of Tyr (Chultan Chapter)
FACTION
StatusActiveDispositionUnknown (Allied to Moor)TypeReligious
Overview

The Church of Tyr maintains a temple in Port Nyanzaru and has a presence within the Chultan temple itself -- Moor recognized the symbol of Tyr above a door in the ancient temple, suggesting historical connections between Tyr's faithful and the temple builders. [CONFIRMED] The Crocodile's Judgment Scale (Scales of Justice) -- a Lawful Good item tied to Tyr -- was found in the temple, further reinforcing this connection. [CONFIRMED]

Moor's devotion to Tyr is functional and active. He used Channel Divinity in Tyr's name and instinctively claimed the Scales. [CONFIRMED]

Golden Snake Idol
ITEM
StatusIn Fizzleblast's Possession (Missing One Eye)RarityLegendaryTypeQuest Object / ArtifactOwnerFizzleblast
A golden serpent coiled around a sun disk, its jeweled eyes catching the last light of a dying sun -- and one hand, quick and faithless, reached for what should never have been taken.
Physical DescriptionSolid gold, snake-coiled around a sun disk. Heavy (10+ inventory slots). Two jeweled eyes -- one of which has been secretly removed by Aramil. Warm from the focused sunlight. Hung from an archway above the sleeping Tyrannosaur. [CONFIRMED]
PropertiesThe idol is the temple's anchor -- the binding that connects the solar mechanism to whatever divine or arcane principle sustains the sun's relationship with the temple (and possibly the world). With both eyes intact, the sun rises. With one eye removed, the sun forgets. [CONFIRMED -- inscription + observed consequence]
Current StatusDelivered to Fizzleblast. Missing the right eye (the Eye of the Serpent). Fizzleblast has noted: “Why does the idol feel lighter?” [EXTRAPOLATED]
Eye of the Serpent
ITEM
StatusIn Aramil's Possession (Secret)RarityLegendaryTypeArtifact Component / Cursed GemOwnerAramil
Warm as a coal that does not burn. Worth five thousand gold. Worth, potentially, everything.
Physical DescriptionA gem the size of a quail's egg, possibly chrysoberyl or alexandrite variant. Warm -- warmer than body temperature, warmer than the jungle. [CONFIRMED]
PropertiesThe Eye is the functional heart of the Golden Snake Idol. Its removal severed the connection between the idol and the sun, plunging all of Faerun into permanent darkness and causing the undead to rise. [CONFIRMED]
Estimated Value~5,000 gold (per Aramil's assessment), though its true value is incalculable. [CONFIRMED]
Current StatusHidden in Aramil's breast pocket, next to his purple crystal necklace. No one in the party knows. [CONFIRMED]
Staff of Healing
ITEM
StatusIn Party PossessionRarityRareTypeWondrous ItemOwnerMoor
Properties (Demonstrated)10 charges. Used for Cure Wounds, Lesser Restoration (cured Opal's blindness), and Mass Cure Wounds. Moor used multiple charges throughout the session. [CONFIRMED]
SignificanceThe single most impactful item in the session. Without it, Moor could not have sustained the 148 HP of total healing that kept the party alive. [CONFIRMED]
Belt of Hill Giant Strength
ITEM
StatusIn Party PossessionRarityRare to Very RareTypeWondrous ItemOwnerOpalAKABelt of Hill Giant Strength
Properties (Demonstrated)Sets Strength to 23 (+6 modifier). Found by Bob specifically for Opal. Massive upgrade to her primary stat. [CONFIRMED]
SignificanceTransformed Opal from a strong Barbarian into a devastatingly strong Barbarian. Bob finding this for someone else despite his “just here for the loot” persona is a defining character moment. [CONFIRMED]
Cloak of the Bat
ITEM
StatusIn Party PossessionRarityRareTypeWondrous ItemOwnerBob
Properties (Demonstrated)Advantage on Stealth (Dexterity) checks. Fly speed 40 ft in dim light or darkness (both hands must hold the cloak). [CONFIRMED]
AcquisitionBob rolled a Nat 1 on his loot search, invoked Halfling Lucky, rerolled to 18, and got the Cloak. “I am a halfling. I'm lucky.” [CONFIRMED]
SignificanceMade the Golden Snake Idol heist possible. Bob flew from the archway after stealing the idol, surviving the rival party's Fireball mid-flight. [CONFIRMED]
Scales of Justice (Crocodile's Judgment Scale)
ITEM
StatusIn Party PossessionRarityRareTypeWondrous ItemOwnerMoorAKACrocodile's Judgment Scale
Properties (Stated)Set of scales on a pressure plate pedestal. Allows one full die re-roll per long rest for anyone in the party. Requires Lawful Good alignment. [CONFIRMED]
AcquisitionMoor grabbed it (“No, I grab it”) while Bob performed a sleight-of-hand swap (rolled 19 with Guidance) to replace it with oil flasks on the pressure plate. [CONFIRMED]
SignificanceA Lawful Good item claimed by the party's Lawful Good cleric of Tyr, in a session where another party member committed an act of world-ending selfishness. The thematic irony is profound. [EXTRAPOLATED]
Zephyr Reliquary
ITEM
StatusIn Party PossessionRarityRareTypeWondrous ItemOwnerLavender
Properties (Demonstrated)Small jar with faint blue mist. Grants immunity to fire damage. Was functioning as the temple's “air conditioning” -- removing it caused the temple's internal temperature to rise, attracting baboons and increasing environmental fire damage. [CONFIRMED]
SignificanceLavender's MVP performance was built on this item. Fire immunity allowed her to walk through the mirror beams unharmed, move snake statues in areas of concentrated heat, and generally operate in zones lethal to everyone else. [CONFIRMED]

A chronological record of significant events from Fizzleblast's Fieldtrip, compiled from the session transcript.
Session 1 -- Fizzleblast's Fieldtrip
LORE
Event 1.1: Arrival on the Chultan Shore
TypeTravel
LocationThe Shore / Fizzle Field
What HappenedThe party arrived by canoe on the black sand shore of the Chultan jungle. Fizzleblast delivered a hurried briefing: retrieve the golden snake artifact from the temple, keep all other loot, watch out for rivals one hour behind them. Niles tripped on a crab and flung the Bag of Holding, shattering the Potion of Instant Attunement and randomly binding magical equipment to each party member.
ParticipantsMoor, Opal, Aramil, Lavender, Bob, Fizzleblast, Niles
ConsequencesEach PC received random magical equipment. Fizzleblast retreated to the Fizzle Field bubble. The clock started.
Event 1.2: Gorallon Encounter
TypeCombat / Social
LocationTemple Entrance
What HappenedTwo Gorallons blocked the temple entrance. Moor redirected a thermal mirror beam to melt the first Gorallon. Aramil used Speak with Animals to intimidate the second into fleeing (“Think about your wife and kids”).
ParticipantsMoor, Aramil
ConsequencesTemple entrance cleared. Moor established the mirror mechanic as a combat tool. Aramil demonstrated his negotiation-first approach.
Event 1.3: The Antechamber of Blades
TypeDiscovery
LocationAntechamber of Blades
What HappenedThe party solved the Man-and-Crocodile pressure plate puzzle. Moor and Lavender walked the plates in synchronized steps. Bob bypassed the plates entirely by crawling on the ceiling with Slippers of Spider Climbing. The Scales of Justice were retrieved via Bob's sleight-of-hand swap.
ParticipantsFull party
ConsequencesInner temple accessed. Scales of Justice claimed by Moor (Lawful Good alignment requirement noted).
Event 1.4: Opal's Blinding
TypeLoss
LocationPrism Room
What HappenedOpal attempted to push a mirror and rolled a natural 1. She fell into the focused beam, taking 14 radiant damage directly to her eyes. She went blind. Lavender tied a bandana over her eyes. Moor determined the blindness would pass with time or healing magic.
ParticipantsOpal, Lavender, Moor
ConsequencesOpal fought the remainder of the Prism Room encounter blind. This set up the session's most dramatic combat moment.
Event 1.5: Poison Gas Attack
TypeCombat (Environmental)
LocationPrism Room
What HappenedA dead baboon fell onto a pressure plate, triggering noxious gas vents. The entire party took 14 poison damage. Moor used the Staff of Healing for emergency healing. Lavender cast Gust of Wind to disperse the gas.
ParticipantsFull party
ConsequencesParty resources strained significantly. Moor began his 148 HP healing marathon. Lavender's utility casting established her MVP trajectory.
Event 1.6: The Giant Constrictor Snake
TypeCombat
LocationPrism Room
What HappenedA forty-foot Giant Constrictor Snake emerged from a stone snake statue. Aramil attempted to persuade it to eat monkeys instead -- failed. The snake killed Tika (Lavender's familiar) and constricted Aramil for 22 damage, dropping him to -10 HP. Moor healed Aramil. Opal, blind, killed the snake with a critical hit greataxe strike for 30 damage.
ParticipantsFull party
ConsequencesAramil nearly died. Tika was killed (resummoned later). Opal's blind kill became the session's signature combat moment.
Notable DetailsOpal heard the snake's scales and felt the vibration through the stone. She located it through sound alone. The critical hit sheared through multiple coils.
Event 1.7: Temple Puzzle Completion
TypeDiscovery
LocationPrism Room / Snake Sanctum
What HappenedLavender (fire immune via Zephyr Reliquary) walked through the beams and helped Opal (vision restored by Moor's Lesser Restoration) redirect the snake statues to light all four gems. The portcullis opened. Moor's Turn Undead destroyed 7 of 9 skeletons behind a blocked door.
ParticipantsLavender, Opal, Moor
ConsequencesAccess to the T-Rex Courtyard. Second wave of loot acquired.
Event 1.8: The Idol Heist
TypeDiscovery / Turning Point
LocationT-Rex Courtyard
What HappenedBob climbed the archway above the sleeping Tyrannosaur, stole the Golden Snake Idol (stealth 20, then 21; sleight of hand 22), and flew out using the Cloak of the Bat. The rival party launched a Fireball that missed Bob (half damage, 9 HP) but woke the T-Rex.
ParticipantsBob (primary), Moor (Light cantrip support)
ConsequencesIdol secured. T-Rex awakened. Escape sequence triggered. The session's climactic moment.
Event 1.9: The Escape
TypeCombat / Travel
LocationEntire temple, then The Shore
What Happened4 minutes 20 seconds to reach the Fizzle Field. Aramil athletics-checked through the closing door. Lavender vaulted the gas trap. Bob jumped 36 feet across pressure plates. Opal and Moor hauled counterweight swords. Two party members took 13 damage each from blade traps. Two rival mages appeared via Dimension Door; Aramil's Phantasmal Force (raptors) caused one to roll a Nat 1 and flee, dragging his ally. Moor's Command (“Flee”) hit the other.
ParticipantsFull party vs. rival party
ConsequencesParty reached safety. Rivals fled into the jungle. Undead rose outside the bubble. Rival screams heard, then silence.
Event 1.10: Aramil's Theft of the Eye
TypeTurning Point
LocationThe Shore
What HappenedWhile the party rested and Fizzleblast slept, Aramil secretly pried the Eye of the Serpent from the Golden Snake Idol using his Gloves of Thievery (sleight of hand 18). No one saw.
ParticipantsAramil (alone)
ConsequencesThe sun did not rise. Eternal darkness fell on Faerun. The undead rose across the continent. No one in the party knows Aramil is responsible.
Event 1.11: Dawn Fails
TypeTurning Point / Loss
LocationThe Shore / Fizzle Field
